# Loop Patterns Across the Stack {#sec-loop-patterns-across-stack}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"There is no single development, in either technology or management technique, which by itself promises even one order of magnitude improvement in productivity, in reliability, in simplicity."*
>
> — Fred Brooks, *No Silver Bullet* (1986)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Fred Brooks, Turing Award winner and author of *The Mythical Man-Month*, famously warned against hoping for a "silver bullet" in software engineering. For us, this is a reminder that there is no monolithic AI solution for the entire computing stack; different layers, from compiler to RTL to SoC, often need different feedback budgets, rejection checks, and commitment boundaries.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{This chapter makes two linked moves. First, it applies the same design-loop card across workload characterization, fast software loops, architecture design-space exploration, domain-specific architecture and code generation, hardware/software co-design, deployed systems, and high-commitment RTL or physical-design flows. These are operating regimes distinguished by feedback cost, rollback cost, evidence burden, rejection authority, and commitment level. Second, the chapter derives a rejection-bound service-cost model for the point at which trusted disposition, rather than candidate generation, limits progress.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What does the same design-loop card reveal across operating regimes, and when
does trusted disposition become the throughput limit?
:::

The previous chapter ran one bounded turn of the lighthouse prompt and showed
the card and illustrative supporting evidence it leaves behind. This chapter asks whether the same card can read
other loops across the stack, from cheap software feedback to high-commitment
physical evidence. If the framework only describes one kind of design-space-exploration loop,
it is too narrow. If it describes everything in the same way, it is too vague.
The useful middle ground is a set of loop patterns.

> **Loop pattern.** A loop pattern is a recurring shape of AI-assisted
> architecture work in which a generative method or automated optimizer acts on represented state under
> a characteristic feedback budget, evidence burden, rejection authority, and
> commitment level. The patterns in this chapter locate operating regimes; they
> do not prescribe one method posture for every loop at a stack layer.

Workload characterization is one pattern. Fast compiler and runtime tuning is
another, as are accelerator, memory, interconnect, and chiplet exploration;
domain-specific architecture and code generation, because local hardware
efficiency only matters if the software path can reach it; co-design across
compute, memory, network, and power; fleet and serving systems; and RTL,
physical design, and signoff. The ontology is the same, but the evidence burden
changes.

This distinction protects the book from two mistakes. The first mistake is to
pretend that every architecture task can be automated like a fast software
loop. The second is to become so conservative that Architecture 2.0 is only a
new name for old design review. The right question is more precise. Given this
task, representation, environment, feedback budget, and commitment level, what
method roles are useful, what evidence is credible, and what can reject the
result?

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can choose the AI-assisted loop pattern before choosing the AI method.
That means you can:

- **Compare** operating regimes with the same card fields rather than as isolated anecdotes;
- **Use** method posture as a supporting lens, not a competing stack taxonomy;
- **Declare** the automation authority, accountable decision owner, and unsupported claim boundary;
- **Estimate** rejection-bound service cost with matched audits and explicit escalation costs.
:::

## A Template for Reading the Cases

The cases in this chapter are read through one card schema. The card asks for
intent, task, design space, representation, environment, method role, feedback
budget, supporting evidence, failed runs, rejected alternatives, rejection
authority, commitment boundary, and decision owner. This keeps the chapter from becoming a list of examples.
It also lets the reader compare loops that otherwise look unrelated. The
lighthouse prompt remains the spine.
When a separate benchmark, tool, or paper appears, it is used as a controlled
slice of the prompt (workload coverage, code generation, architecture search,
deployment feedback, or high-commitment physical evidence), not as a competing
running example. This chapter also carries the measured deployment-facing
fleet-migration example promised in @sec-running-the-loop; it is not presented as
a measured hardware co-design loop.

Keep one conditional rule in view as the cases become more technical. Once
proposal arrival exceeds trusted disposition capacity, adding candidates no
longer scales that adjudication stage. Capacity improves when validated cheap
checks can settle more comparable cases, or when routing, audits, escalation,
reference checks, or concurrency improve. The end of the chapter states this as
a scoped service-cost model, while the cases supply the intuition.

The chapter's primary comparison axis is feedback latency and cost together
with typical rollback cost and decision consequence (@fig-loop-pattern-spectrum). The
boxes are not a maturity scale or an absolute ordering. Fast software studies are not better than
silicon-facing studies, and stack position does not determine commitment by itself.
They are illustrative operating regimes for the same ontology.

![**Loop patterns share an ontology but differ in operating regime:** Every pattern still connects a bounded study, representation and environment, AI method roles, evidence, an architectural claim, and an accountable decision. Typical feedback latency, rollback cost, review mechanism, evidence burden, and delegated authority differ, but stack position does not assign commitment automatically.](images/F10-loop-pattern-spectrum){#fig-loop-pattern-spectrum width="100%" fig-alt="Comparison of illustrative loop-pattern operating regimes by feedback latency, rollback cost, review mechanism, evidence burden, and delegated authority, with a shared ontology that includes the architectural claim and accountable decision."}

The figure should be read horizontally as a comparison, not a progression. A fast
software study may allow more automation when feedback is cheap and rollback
is local. A silicon-facing study may use the same ontology but demand stronger
evidence, independent rejection, and a tighter commitment boundary. The actual
decision consequence and delegated authority still have to be declared for the study.

The compact comparison in @tbl-loop-patterns applies that axis to seven recurring
stack patterns. The names help locate an operating regime; they do not classify
every paper or force every task at one layer into the same contract. Read the
method column as declared automation authority for an assistant, optimizer,
critic, or repair tool.

| **Loop pattern** | **Feedback and rollback regime** | **Useful method work** | **Rejection and commitment** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Workload and benchmark | Trace and coverage checks can be repeated; scope can be revised before claims harden. | Generate, cluster, summarize, and test workload questions. | Coverage gaps, leakage, or irrelevant metrics bound generalization. |
| Fast software | Tests and profiles are fast; rollback is usually local and cheap. | Automate bounded search, tuning, and repair. | Tests, regressions, and deployment guards reject changes before wider rollout. |
| Architecture DSE and specialization | Proxies and simulations are scarcer; discarding a candidate is cheap, but implementation is not. | Generate, predict, optimize, critique, and preserve failed runs and rejected alternatives. | Invalid actions, proxy mismatch, software incompatibility, or weak Pareto evidence block escalation. |
| Domain-specific architecture and code generation | Kernel checks are cheap; interface and maintenance commitments are expensive. | Generate mappings, tune schedules, explain portability limits, and critique software-path gaps. | Correctness, portability, interface cost, and maintenance evidence reject local-only wins. |
| Co-design | Feedback spans layers and latencies; rollback is coupled across interfaces. | Coordinate methods across layers and expose tradeoffs. | System objectives reject single-layer wins. |
| Systems and fleet | Telemetry is timely but noisy and confounded; rollback is guarded, not free. | Adapt, monitor, critique, and revise policy under declared limits. | SLOs, safety policies, rollback triggers, and accountable operations review stop rollout. |
| RTL and physical | Tool feedback is slow and costly; rollback becomes harder toward silicon. | Narrow search, organize evidence, critique, and repair bounded artifacts. | Tool flows, signoff, and the named commitment owner bound automation. |

: **Different loop patterns need different evidence postures:** Workload, software, design-space, code-generation, co-design, fleet, and RTL/physical-design loops use the same fields but differ in feedback latency, rejection authority, rollback cost, and commitment boundary. {#tbl-loop-patterns tbl-colwidths="[17,26,25,22]"}

```{=latex}
\Needspace{11\baselineskip}
```

The cases that follow should be read through the same three questions:

- **Physical constraints and rollback costs:** Which constraints apply, what
  does rollback cost, and what blast radius follows if the case goes wrong? A
  compiler flag, runtime policy, RTL edit, chiplet boundary, and fleet deployment
  do not carry the same blast radius.
- **Action-observation mapping:** What can the loop change, and what tool, trace,
  log, or measurement does it receive in return?
- **Rejection checks and accountability:** What can reject the result, where
  does accountable review occur, and what are the declared automation limits?

A case is not credible because it uses an advanced method.
It is credible when the allowed actions, feedback source, evidence burden,
and rejection authority match the commitment being made.

The rejection path can also carry a blind spot that only scale reveals.

::: {.callout-field-note title="The cores that quietly did the math wrong"}
At fleet scale, Google and Meta independently found that a small number of CPU cores silently miscompute, returning wrong results with no crash and no error flag, and that these mercurial cores slip through manufacturing test and surface only across millions of machines [@HochschildEtAl2021Cores; @DixitEtAl2021SDC]. Meta traced a real corruption to missing files from one bad core, at a rate on the order of one in a thousand devices. The gate everyone trusted, the manufacturing test, never covered a failure that is silent by definition, so nothing rejected it until scale made it visible.

**Lesson:** A rejection gate is only as good as the failure class it can see. At fleet scale the loop needs continuous, cross-checking rejection, because even the axiom every higher layer assumed, that the hardware computes correctly, is a claim that has to be verified.
:::

For a new project, use @tbl-loop-pattern-decision to locate the operating regime
before choosing the method. The table applies the primary comparison; it does
not introduce another taxonomy.

| **If the bottleneck is...** | **Start with this loop pattern** | **First supporting-evidence artifact to build** | **Do not claim yet** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Unknown workload coverage, stale benchmarks, or unclear scenario boundaries. | Workload and benchmark. | Versioned workload packet with coverage, gaps, and drift notes. | A general architecture win. |
| Fast code, compiler, runtime, or kernel feedback. | Fast software. | Testable code path with correctness, profiling, and rollback evidence. | Hardware/system superiority from a local speedup. |
| Many candidate architecture knobs under scarce simulation. | Architecture DSE and specialization. | Candidate records, action schema, proxy calibration, and rejected regions. | Implementation readiness. |
| Local hardware efficiency gated by software path, code generation, or interface cost. | Domain-specific architecture and code generation. | Executable software-path packet with kernels, IR/runtime path, interface-cost model, tests, and portability checks. | End-to-end architecture win. |
| Cross-layer tradeoffs across workload, compiler, memory, network, power, or deployment. | Co-design. | Interface map that names layer owners, changed assumptions, and rejection checks. | A single-layer optimum as a system result. |
| Live or deployment-like behavior, SLOs, canaries, or drift. | Systems and fleet. | Guarded telemetry packet with rollback and policy constraints. | Clean causal evidence without controls. |
| RTL, generators, physical design, signoff, or silicon-facing decisions. | RTL and physical. | Evidence record with tool-stage checks, waivers, and accountable review. | Commitment beyond declared automation authority. |

: **Choose the loop pattern before choosing the method:** The first loop artifact
should match the bottleneck and prevent the loop from overclaiming beyond its
evidence. {#tbl-loop-pattern-decision tbl-colwidths="[26,18,30,16]"}

### A Supporting Method-Posture Lens

Stack position and operating regime remain the primary comparison. A secondary
lens describes how a method interacts with feedback inside any row of
@tbl-loop-patterns. These postures recur across the stack and do not form a
second taxonomy.

- **Surrogate prediction** uses a fast proxy to triage expensive feedback and
  fails when proxy mismatch goes undetected.
- **Constrained optimization** searches legal actions under explicit rejection
  checks. Reinforcement learning[^fn-rl-environment-contract] or another optimizer may explore the
  space, while the environment contract bounds its actions.
- **Generator-critic repair** lets a generator, such as an LLM[^fn-ml-llm-generator-critic], propose a
  bounded artifact and use compiler, simulator, formal, or synthesis failures
  to guide another attempt.

[^fn-rl-environment-contract]: In a constrained optimization loop, a reward signal can guide search only inside legal actions; the environment contract decides which moves are invalid and which evidence can reject a candidate.

[^fn-ml-llm-generator-critic]: In this loop posture, the LLM is only the generator. The architecture value comes from the repair loop around it, where compiler, simulator, formal, or synthesis feedback rejects bounded artifacts and returns traces for the next attempt.

The cases below stay organized by operating regime. The posture lens only names
the method-feedback interaction within a case.

## Workload Characterization and Benchmark Construction

Workload characterization is not a prelude to architecture work. It is
architecture work. The workload defines what behavior matters, which metrics
are meaningful, which software stack is assumed, and which design choices can
be justified. Classic workload-characterization work made this concrete by
measuring program behavior, comparing benchmark suites, and separating
inherent workload properties from artifacts of a particular machine
[@HosteEeckhout2007MICA]. In Architecture 2.0, that lineage becomes loop
state. Trace collection, benchmark construction, workload generation,
clustering, summarization, coverage analysis, drift detection, and explicit
questions about what the benchmark does not represent are all loop state.

The lighthouse prompt makes this concrete. "XRBench-class real-time mobile XR
workload" is not a magic input string. XRBench, a benchmarking suite designed for extended reality, gives the loop a benchmark anchor
[@KwonEtAl2023XRBench], but the architecture question still depends on
which XR workloads, models, devices, frame-rate targets, latency constraints,
memory behaviors, and software paths are represented. A loop that optimizes
one benchmark point may miss the distribution that a real mobile XR subsystem
must serve.
For an AI-assisted loop, the workload arrives as a packet, not a name. This packet includes scenario
labels, device class, trace provenance, input distributions, excluded cases,
coverage gaps, and rejection conditions.

MLPerf, the industry-standard machine learning benchmark suite, is the standing example; @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales treated it as
maintained community infrastructure with versions, rules, and submission
practices [@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. The point to carry into a loop pattern is not another benchmark history. The
loop-pattern lesson is that a benchmark is a living agreement about what evidence should
count. In active ML-serving domains, workload drift can be rapid, for example shifts from dense Transformers to Mixture-of-Experts[^fn-ml-moe-workload-drift] in months. A static benchmark can become a liability, so an Architecture 2.0 loop needs an explicit refresh path for new graphs, traces, and coverage claims. A workload loop should therefore record benchmark version, workload
source, coverage claims, known gaps, leakage risks, and the conditions under
which a result should not generalize.

[^fn-ml-moe-workload-drift]: The MoE point here is workload drift. A model-family shift can change communication, memory, and scheduling pressure fast enough that a static benchmark needs an explicit refresh path.

The reframe becomes explicit when traditional tasks are set against their
Architecture 2.0 equivalents (@tbl-workload-characterization-20). The
left column is still necessary. Representative workloads, profiles, benchmark
construction, and performance comparison remain central to architecture. The
right columns state what changes when an automated optimizer is allowed to act
inside the loop. The workload must become represented state, and the loop must
know what evidence can reject a candidate that only wins a stale, narrow, or
leaky workload slice.

| **Architecture 1.0 meaning** | **Architecture 2.0 meaning** | **Loop state required** | **Failure if missing** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Select representative workloads or benchmark suites. | Define the versioned workload distribution the loop is allowed to optimize over. | Scenario metadata, input distributions, versions, provenance, and inclusion/exclusion rationale. | The loop optimizes one stale or convenient slice and reports a false win. |
| Profile behavior: locality, branch behavior, memory traffic, bandwidth, latency, energy, and phase behavior. | Expose workload features that can drive prediction, search, critique, and active test selection. | Feature schema, measurement provenance, tool configuration, uncertainty, and known blind spots. | A predictor learns a proxy that does not survive another phase, input, or fidelity level. |
| Compare architectures under a fixed benchmark. | Maintain an evidence record across workload variants, candidate designs, and fidelity levels. | Candidate IDs, workload IDs, simulator/tool versions, feedback cost, accepted results, and rejected results. | Design-space results cannot be audited or reused by another loop. |
| Build or curate benchmarks for community comparison. | Define an environment contract: valid tasks, inputs, actions, metrics, leakage rules, and rejection checks. | Benchmark harness, validity checks, metric definitions, seeds, test splits, and update policy. | The loop overfits benchmark artifacts or takes actions outside the intended task. |
| Explain why a workload matters. | Make workload intent, deployment context, and drift explicit enough for the named owner to accept or reject a claim. | Use-case assumptions, deployment constraints, quality-of-service targets, telemetry hooks, and review notes. | The loop produces a plausible result for the wrong product or deployment regime. |
| Summarize results for a paper. | Preserve workload evidence as reusable architecture data. | Evidence record, failed runs, rejected alternatives, and rationale for final claims. | The next loop repeats invalid experiments or loses why prior choices were rejected. |

: **Workload characterization becomes represented loop state:** Classic profiling and benchmark construction remain necessary, but an Architecture 2.0 loop must also record versions, provenance, coverage, rejection conditions, and failed runs. {#tbl-workload-characterization-20 tbl-colwidths="[20,26,25,20]"}

The method roles in this loop are often not glamorous. Those roles are valuable
because they improve the question the architecture loop is answering.

Read as a loop card, the pattern is compact. The task is workload definition;
the representation is versioned traces, metadata, and coverage notes; the
environment is a benchmark harness with update rules; the method posture is
clustering, summarization, generation of missing cases, and critique; the
rejection authority is coverage, leakage, drift, or irrelevant metrics; the
commitment boundary is whether a design claim may generalize beyond the measured
slice.

## Fast Software Loops

Moving from workload definition to code generation, fast software loops sit near the low-commitment end of the spectrum. Compiler
flags, kernels, library implementations, runtime policies, configuration
settings, and small code repairs can often be evaluated quickly and rolled
back. Feedback may come from unit tests, microbenchmarks, integration tests,
profilers, telemetry, or canary deployment[^fn-systems-canary-deployment].

[^fn-systems-canary-deployment]: A canary deployment is a controlled, partial rollout that exposes a change to a small slice of traffic to catch regressions before a system-wide release. It is one of the cheap, reversible rejectors that let a fast software loop iterate quickly, since a bad change can be caught and rolled back before it becomes a wider commitment.

This is the regime where stronger automation is often plausible. Autotuning
systems and learned tensor-program optimizers[^fn-ml-tensor-program-optimizers] show how search spaces, cost
models, measurements, and scheduling can be combined to improve software
performance across targets
[@ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM; @ZhengEtAl2020Ansor]. An Architecture 2.0 loop can
learn from that pattern without pretending that all hardware design is equally
reversible.

[^fn-ml-tensor-program-optimizers]: Tensor-program optimizers automate the search for efficient execution schedules of tensor operations on a target, using cost models and on-device measurements to steer the search. They are a template a fast software loop can borrow, since their feedback is cheap enough to support aggressive automated search without treating every hardware decision as equally reversible.

Kernel-generation benchmarks make the same point in a current form.
KernelBench, a dataset and environment for generating low-level CUDA kernels, evaluates whether models can produce GPU kernels that are both
correct and faster than a baseline [@OuyangEtAl2025KernelBench]. This is
a fast software loop because correctness tests, compilation, profiling, and
microbenchmark feedback are close to the generated artifact. It also touches
hardware/software co-design because performance depends on memory layout,
parallelism, numerical precision, backend behavior, and target-specific
hardware resources. Multi-platform kernel-generation work (often targeting GPU-programming layers like OpenAI's Triton, a Python-embedded GPU-kernel DSL, or NVIDIA's CUDA parallel-computing platform) makes that bridge
explicit by separating the core benchmark from target backends
[@WenEtAl2025MultiKernelBench].
For an automated optimizer, the benchmark object needs more than a prompt and a score, requiring kernel
IDs, input-shape distributions, correctness oracles, target metadata,
failed compiles and profiles, and rejected variants.

Read as a loop card, the task is bounded code generation or tuning; the
representation is source code, compiler IR, tests, target metadata, and
profiling output; the environment is the compiler/runtime/profiler path; the
method posture can be more automated because feedback is cheap; the rejection
authority is correctness, compilation, portability, performance regression, and
deployment maintainability; the commitment boundary arrives when a local kernel
change becomes part of a larger software or hardware contract.

The reason declared automation authority can be wider here is not that the task is easy. It is
that failures are often observable, bounded, and reversible. A generated
kernel can be tested. A compiler flag can be reverted. A runtime policy can be
canaried. A regression can be caught by a benchmark or deployment guard.
Because rejection is close to the action, the loop can iterate quickly.

::: {.callout-failure-mode title="Fast feedback is not complete truth"}
Treating fast feedback as complete truth is the trap for the optimizer. An AI-generated kernel that
wins on one input size may regress another. An AI-proposed runtime policy that improves
average latency may worsen tail latency. Even in fast AI-assisted loops, the
card still needs workload coverage, rejection authority, and a named decision
owner when the change affects a larger system.
:::

## Architecture Loops: Accelerators, Memory, and Chiplets

Moving beyond software, architecture design-space exploration represents the canonical middle case in terms of commitment and feedback cost. The loop
may explore accelerator organization, vector width, cache hierarchy, local
memory, interconnect, chiplet partitioning, and package assumptions. It may
also choose how work is divided across CPUs, accelerators, and SoC blocks.
Feedback is slower than software tests and less definitive than silicon.
Actions can be invalid. Proxies can lie. The space is too large for exhaustive
enumeration.

This is where the Architecture 2.0 framework feels most natural. The task is
bounded but rich. The representation must expose architectural state. The
environment must define legal actions and observations. Methods can generate
candidates, predict behavior, optimize evaluations, critique assumptions, and
preserve failed runs and rejected alternatives. ArchGym, an open-source framework connecting machine learning algorithms to architecture simulators, is one example of making such loops more
explicit for machine-learning-assisted architecture design
[@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]; predictive design-space-exploration work
shows that data-driven modeling has a longer architecture lineage
[@IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE]. Transferable frameworks such as Apollo, which provides a unified search space over different accelerator architectures, go a step
further, training a surrogate on an architecture dataset and carrying the same
search across different design spaces [@yazdanbakhsh2021apollo].
That dataset has to be treated as a loop artifact, not a generic training set. Its
design-space schema, sampled-region coverage, tool versions, invalid-action
labels, rejected regions, and proxy-fidelity calibration determine what a generative method
may infer from it.

Chiplets raise the stakes because they turn partitioning and interfaces into
legal actions inside the loop. A UCIe-class interface, referring to the Universal Chiplet Interconnect Express standard, should be represented as
an environment contract that specifies what may be split across dies, what
bandwidth/latency/power/thermal feedback tools return, which package and
software assumptions are fixed, and which candidates must escalate or be
rejected when interface evidence is missing [@UCIeConsortium2026Spec].

:::: {.callout-lighthouse title="The subsystem choice is an integration choice"}
**Context.** In an AI-assisted architecture DSE loop, "CPU extension," "accelerator," and
"SoC block" are not interchangeable labels for the AI to explore.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** "64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem" and
"vector-capable CPU, accelerator, or SoC block" ask where the "XRBench-class
real-time mobile XR workload" lives relative to the core, memory hierarchy,
interconnect, compiler/runtime path, and product boundary. If the optimizer proposes a vector extension, it
changes the CPU contract; if it proposes a tightly coupled accelerator, it changes the invocation,
sharing, and memory-attachment contract.

**Integration boundary.** The method may still test a looser accelerator variant,
but the loop environment must make the coupling assumption explicit so the optimizer cannot silently break integration contracts.
::::

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Cross-Layer Rejection Check"}
When an optimizer tunes against a local proxy such as core area, the loop must
enforce its cross-layer boundary. Does the environment reject candidates that
win only by pushing complexity into memory traffic, compiler support,
verification, or SoC integration?
:::

Read as a loop card, the task is architecture search; the representation is
candidate parameters, workload state, constraints, tool configurations, and
prior rejected regions; the environment is a simulator, mapper, compiler path,
or staged DSE harness; the method posture is generation, prediction,
optimization, and critique; the rejection authority is invalid actions,
simulator mismatch, power or software incompatibility, and weak Pareto evidence;
the commitment boundary is escalation to stronger feedback.

## Domain-Specific Architecture and Code Generation

Specialization can improve efficiency, but a domain is not one knob. It may be
a kernel or model family, data type, memory pattern, programming model,
deployment regime, latency envelope, product vertical, or software ecosystem.
The golden-age argument for specialization [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]
therefore becomes a loop question. Which properties are stable enough to
specialize, and which must remain programmable?

The first rejection test prices the interface. Amdahl's law
[@Amdahl1967ValiditySingleProcessor], its multicore reinterpretation
[@HillMarty2008AmdahlMulticore], and the accelerator-focused LogCA model
[@AltafWood2017LogCA] all prevent a local speedup from standing in for an
end-to-end result. A compact form that keeps interface and software cost visible
is
$$
S_{\mathrm{system}} \le
\frac{1}{(1-f) + f/s + \epsilon_{\mathrm{interface}} +
\epsilon_{\mathrm{software}}}.
$$
Here, $f$ is the improvable fraction of baseline time, $s$ is local speedup,
and the $\epsilon$ terms are interface and software overheads normalized to the
same baseline. The mechanism pays only when enough work per invocation
amortizes the path to it. In an AI-assisted loop, the equation is a rejection
contract. A proposed specialization needs interface, software-path, and
end-to-end evidence. A related service-cost form returns for adjudication in
@sec-the-rejection-bound.

A simplified LogCA-style calculation in @fig-logca-breakeven makes the interface
tax visible [@AltafWood2017LogCA]. End-to-end speedup appears only after work per
offload clears a break-even point set by latency and overhead.

```{python}
#| label: fig-logca-breakeven
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **The interface sets a break-even granularity:** In a simplified LogCA-style model [@AltafWood2017LogCA], end-to-end speedup rises with the work amortized per offload only after it clears a break-even granularity set by offload latency and overhead, then saturates at the accelerator's peak acceleration. The farther the accelerator sits from the host, the larger the granularity needed before offload even breaks even. Curves are illustrative; the lesson is the break-even cliff, not the exact values.
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Line plot showing offload speedup curves that rise only after enough work is amortized to overcome interface latency and overhead."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style

apply_style()

A = 100.0  # peak acceleration (asymptote)
g = [10 ** (6 * i / 399) for i in range(400)]

curves = [
    {"label": "tightly coupled accelerator", "ovh": 2.0, "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"label": "shared-memory SoC block", "ovh": 40.0, "color": COLORS["green"]},
    {"label": "off-chip / discrete module", "ovh": 1500.0, "color": COLORS["red"]},
]

def speedup(gran, ovh):
    # LogCA: Speedup(g) = C*g / (o + L + C*g/A), with C = 1, beta = 1, (o + L) = ovh
    return gran / (ovh + gran / A)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.25, 3.12))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.12, right=0.86, top=0.82, bottom=0.28)

ax.axhline(A, color=COLORS["ink"], linewidth=0.8, linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=1)
ax.text(1.35, A * 1.08, "peak acceleration A", ha="left", va="bottom", fontsize=6.2, color=COLORS["ink"])
ax.axhline(1.0, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.7, zorder=1)
ax.text(7.5e5, 1.10, "break-even", ha="right", va="bottom", fontsize=6.0, color=COLORS["muted"])

ax.axhspan(0.25, 1.0, color="#FDECEC", alpha=0.35, zorder=0)
ax.text(1.5, 0.38, "offload loses", ha="left", va="center", fontsize=6.0, color=COLORS["red"], fontstyle="italic")

for c in curves:
    s = [speedup(x, c["ovh"]) for x in g]
    ax.plot(g, s, color=c["color"], linewidth=1.9, zorder=3, label=c["label"])
    g1 = c["ovh"] / (1.0 - 1.0 / A)
    ax.scatter([g1], [1.0], marker="o", s=22, facecolor=COLORS["note_fill"], edgecolor=c["color"], linewidth=1.3, zorder=4, clip_on=False)

ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlim(1, 1e6)
ax.set_ylim(0.25, 150)
ax.set_xlabel("operation granularity  (work amortized per offload)", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylabel("end-to-end speedup", fontsize=7)
ax.set_yticks([0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100])
ax.set_yticklabels(["0.3x", "1x", "3x", "10x", "30x", "100x"], fontsize=6.4)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2, labelsize=6.4)
ax.grid(axis="both", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.5, zorder=0)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

legend = ax.legend(
    loc="lower left",
    bbox_to_anchor=(0.0, 1.02),
    fontsize=6.0,
    frameon=True,
    facecolor="white",
    edgecolor="white",
    framealpha=0.92,
    handlelength=1.6,
    borderpad=0.15,
    labelspacing=0.3,
    ncol=1,
)
for text in legend.get_texts():
    text.set_color(COLORS["ink"])

add_note_box(
    fig,
    "Simplified LogCA-style view: the farther the accelerator sits from the host, the larger the granularity needed before offload breaks even.",
    xywh=(0.10, 0.03, 0.80, 0.11),
    fontsize=5.5,
)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

@fig-domain-specificity-shapes is a checklist, not another taxonomy. A benchmark
name does not establish a stable domain. The loop must represent the kernel
family, precision contract, data-movement pattern, software interface,
deployment constraints, drift risk, and test coverage because that bundle
determines legal actions and rejectable claims.

![**Domain specificity has many shapes:** A domain-specific architecture is not specialized for a label; it is specialized for a bundle of kernel, precision, data-movement, interface, deployment, drift, and verification constraints. The study must characterize the shape its architectural claim is trying to exploit.](images/F10a-domain-specificity-shapes){#fig-domain-specificity-shapes width="100%" fig-alt="Multi-shape diagram showing that a domain-specific architecture study must characterize kernels, precision, data movement, interfaces, deployment constraints, drift, and verification needs before claiming specialization."}

If the domain shape determines what to build, the software path is the narrow
waist that determines whether the workload can reach it. Halide-style
algorithm/schedule separation, AutoTVM/Ansor-style measured schedules, and
MLIR (Multi-Level Intermediate Representation)-style representations turn reachability into explicit mapping
actions, compiler feedback, and rejection evidence
[@RaganKelleyEtAl2017HalideCACM; @ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM; @ZhengEtAl2020Ansor;
@LattnerEtAl2020MLIR]. @fig-codegen-narrow-waist places programming models,
compiler IRs, libraries, runtimes, and generated code between domain intent and
hardware or deployment evidence.

![**Specialized hardware needs a code-generation waist:** Domain-specific architecture claims must pass through executable software paths and be checked with correctness, performance, portability, and maintainability evidence.](images/F10b-codegen-narrow-waist){#fig-codegen-narrow-waist width="92%" fig-alt="Narrow-waist diagram showing programming models, compiler representations, libraries, runtimes, and generated code connecting specialized hardware to evidence checks."}

Generative methods may work at this waist because kernel-generation benchmarks
keep correctness, compilation, profiling, and target behavior close to the
artifact [@OuyangEtAl2025KernelBench; @WenEtAl2025MultiKernelBench]. Architecture
claims need the wider path. The loop card represents domain shape, kernel
family, data layout, compiler IR, runtime interface, backend capabilities,
tests, portability limits, and maintenance evidence. Its feedback budget is
split. Declared automation may tune cheap, reversible kernel and schedule
choices, while interface commitments require end-to-end evidence and a named
decision owner. Correctness, portability, interface cost, workload drift, and
system measurement can reject the local win.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Hardware-Interface Commitment Boundary"}
Record which kernel and schedule actions automation may commit, which hardware
interface changes require stronger evidence, and who owns that decision. A
cheap kernel win cannot silently widen its authority into an expensive
interface commitment.
:::

::: {.callout-failure-mode title="The accelerator that won in isolation"}
An AI-generated block posts a large kernel speedup, but the compiler cannot keep
it busy, data movement dominates, and only hand-written code reaches the
headline result. The loop measured the wrong thing. Price the interface and
software path, then require a compiler-generated end-to-end measurement before
promoting the candidate.
:::

**Progressive lowering as the architecture loop.** Some domain-specific or
reconfigurable loops make the compiler part of represented design state. A loop
may propose a custom compute unit, IR dialects, and lowering rules within an
explicit software contract. MLIR and CIRCT (Circuit IR Compilers and Tools)
verifiers and legality checks,
simulation, equivalence tests, and synthesis can reject stages of that path.
Passing an early verifier does not establish semantic or physical validity.
@lst-mlir-co-generation shows the record schematically.

```mlir {#lst-mlir-co-generation lst-cap="**Schematic co-generated hardware and software IR:** Pseudo-MLIR showing the loop record, not executable CIRCT/MLIR."}
// Schematic Architecture 2.0 loop record: co-generated HW and SW.
// The loop proposes a hardware dialect and lowering strategy for evaluation.

// 1. The AI defines the hardware intent using CIRCT (Hardware IR)
hw.module @CustomXR_SystolicArray(in %clk: i1, in %act: tensor<16x16xf16>, out %res: tensor<16x16xf16>) {
  // Agent-generated spatial architecture constraints
  %area_est = hw.predict_area(...)
  // Loop rejects if %area_est > mobile_XR_budget
  // Note: Area is heavily approximated here; true IR drop or routing congestion requires physical synthesis.
}

// 2. The AI simultaneously generates the compiler scheduling policy
// using MLIR Transform Dialect to map the XR workload to the new hardware
transform.sequence failures(propagate) {
^bb1(%arg1: !pdl.operation):
  // Agent discovers tiling to 16x16 is optimal for the power envelope
  %0, %loops:2 = transform.structured.tile %arg1 [16, 16]

  // Agent generates the mapping rule to the custom hardware it just designed
  transform.structured.map_to_custom_hw %0 { target = @CustomXR_SystolicArray }
}
```

## Co-Design Loops: Compute, Memory, Network, and Power

While domain-specific loops focus on vertical execution, co-design loops test the oldest claim in this book. Architecture is not only a
layer below software but the place where software behavior, hardware
mechanisms, physical constraints, and system
objectives meet. A single-layer optimization can therefore be locally correct
and globally wrong.

Consider a dataflow choice that reduces compute cycles but increases memory
traffic, a topology change that improves one collective while worsening
another, a cache change that improves average performance but increases tail
latency, or a rack-level policy that saves power while violating service
quality. The loop must represent more than one layer because the objective
lives across layers.

Energy and warehouse-scale coupling matter here because they give the loop cheap
rejectors for cross-layer claims. Data movement, memory locality, network
contention, power and thermal headroom, and deployment policy can all invalidate
a local compute win before expensive implementation evidence is gathered
[@Horowitz2014Energy; @BarrosoHolzleRanganathan2019DatacenterAsComputer].

An Architecture 2.0 co-design loop therefore needs richer representations, such as
workload phase behavior, data movement, memory locality, network behavior,
power and thermal constraints, compiler/runtime choices, and deployment
policy. The useful method roles are coordination and critique as much as
search. The loop should ask which layer changed, which objective improved,
which objective worsened, and which evidence would reject a single-layer win.
In an AI-assisted co-design loop, coordination means naming which layer the
automated optimizer may touch, which layer supplies independent feedback, and which owner
accepts the cross-layer tradeoff.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Cross-Layer Ownership"}
Before an optimizer acts, record which layer it may change, which other layer
supplies independent feedback, what automation may commit, and who owns the
cross-layer tradeoff.
:::

Warehouse-scale computing itself, read as a loop card, is a cross-layer
co-design pattern. The task is useful work per constrained resource; the representation is
workload, software, hardware, network, power, cooling, and operations state; the
environment combines models, traces, deployment measurements, and operational
rules; and the rejection authority is a system objective that a local win violates.

Many Architecture 2.0 loops are even less static than this summary suggests.
In early accelerator, compiler, runtime, or system-interface work, the hardware
target and the software path may both be changing. The loop is then not merely
searching a fixed space; it is managing hardware/software co-evolution. A new
instruction, tensor unit, scratchpad policy, data layout, or runtime interface
changes what software can express, while compiler, kernel, and workload
feedback changes which hardware feature is worth keeping. The supporting
evidence must record that co-evolution by tracking what hardware state existed when the software
was generated, which examples or optimization rules were available, which
correctness checks and performance measurements returned, and which hardware
or software assumption was revised after rejection.

The durable lesson is not that code generation solves architecture. It is that
software feedback can become part of architecture evidence when the study keeps
the hardware target, code path, tests, performance counters, rejected variants,
and decision role tied together. Without that record, a strong generated
kernel or library path may only prove that one software artifact matched one
hardware snapshot. Because the hardware side and software side may both change,
the evidence record must preserve the versions, measurements, failed runs, and rejected variants that
connect them (@fig-hardware-software-coevolution). Otherwise, the study cannot say
whether the architecture improved or only whether one generated path happened to
work.

![**Hardware and software co-evolve through a shared study record:** Early architecture work often changes the hardware target and the software path at the same time. The study becomes credible when both sides are versioned and their snapshots, artifacts, tests, metrics, rejected variants, architectural claim, and accountable review are recorded together.](images/F10c-hardware-software-coevolution){#fig-hardware-software-coevolution width="100%" fig-alt="Two-lane diagram showing hardware target state and software path state evolving together through a shared study record and accountable review of an architectural claim."}

## Deployment-Facing Loops

Deployment-facing loops evaluate architecture choices in deployed or
deployment-like contexts, from large-scale software migration to active
operation. They include scheduling, serving, admission control, memory
allocation, power management, placement, rollout policy, fleet telemetry, and
performance isolation. Their feedback can be richer than simulation because it
comes from real systems. It can also be noisier, more confounded, more
privacy-sensitive, and harder to reproduce.
The deployment-facing benchmark lesson from the workload-pattern discussion
still applies here. Evaluation has scenarios, latency constraints, power
envelopes, and system rules, not only a single accuracy or throughput number.
Fleet loops add another layer, consisting of live telemetry, canary policy, rollback, and
operational review.
For automated optimizers acting inside these loops, telemetry is governed data. The schema, cohort
provenance, canary labels, privacy filters, drift detectors, rollback triggers,
failed runs, and rollback outcomes define what the loop may trust.
The packet should also say whether the signal is operationally useful, causally
diagnostic, or merely a warning that earlier evidence has expired. Production
telemetry can recalibrate the next architecture loop, but it rarely proves a
design claim by itself.

The chapter's measured deployment-facing exemplar is Google's warehouse-scale
migration from x86 to Arm [@ChristopherEtAl2025ISAMigration]. It is explicitly
not a hardware co-design case. The target ISA is fixed, and the loop repairs
software so production services can move to it. The reported effort analyzed
more than 38,000 migration commits. An AI-assisted repair tool was evaluated
separately on a selected replay benchmark of 245 historical commits with
identifiable build and test targets. It produced a passing repair for 30 percent
of that benchmark, a directional result rather than a live fleet automation
rate [@ChristopherEtAl2025ISAMigration].

Builds, tests, sanitizers that expose memory-model differences, and production
monitoring can reject regressions, crash loops, or slow jobs. The declared
automation authority ends where those checks cannot settle a repair, and the
case routes to its application owner. The model will age; the represented
migration state, rejection checks, failed cases, and accountable ownership are
the durable parts. @tbl-isa-migration-loop-card reads the case through the same
card used for every other operating regime.

| **Card field** | **Migration reading** |
| --- | --- |
| **Task** | Move production services across an ISA boundary while preserving build, test, and rollout behavior. |
| **Representation** | Migration commits, build targets, tests, sanitizer findings, service ownership, and evicted production cases. |
| **Environment** | Build-and-test repair loop plus production monitoring on the new target. |
| **Method role** | Propose bounded software repairs and route failed cases toward owners. |
| **Independent rejection path** | Build failures, test failures, sanitizer failures, crash loops, slow jobs, and monitor-triggered evictions. |
| **Commitment boundary** | Automated repair can clear only the cases its declared checks settle; application owners receive the exceptions. |

: **ISA migration as a loop card:** The migration loop requires represented
state, independent rejection, preserved failed cases, and application-owned
exceptions. {#tbl-isa-migration-loop-card tbl-colwidths="[24,66]"}

The most instructive result is what the evidence overturned. The team and
application owners expected low-level differences such as floating-point drift,
concurrency, and platform-specific intrinsics to dominate. Most recorded effort
instead involved tests with x86-specific assumptions, build and release systems,
and configuration rollout. The represented loop found work that unaided
architectural intuition mispredicted.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="Migration evidence shows what ISA claims owe"}
**Context.** The migration case shows why the lighthouse phrase "64-bit RISC-V"
is not only a hardware target for an optimizer to select.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** A "64-bit RISC-V-based" path for the "compute
subsystem" owes evidence for generated code, ABI and memory-model assumptions,
compiler/runtime behavior, library support, tests, and deployment rollout.

**Boundary.** Arm and RISC-V are different targets, but the migration case
reveals the software-contract evidence an ISA choice eventually needs. Build,
test, sanitizers, production monitors, and application ownership define the
rejection and escalation path.

**Takeaway.** An automated ISA proposal is credible only when the loop shows
which software survives the contract and which cases reach an accountable
owner.
:::

The design-loop card changes accordingly. The representation must include
operational state, workload drift, service-level objectives[^fn-systems-slo], resource
contention, customer or user constraints, and rollback mechanisms. The
environment may be a simulator, test cluster, replay harness, staging system,
or production system with guardrails. The method may adapt policy, detect
drift, summarize telemetry, or propose a controlled experiment.
For an AI-assisted deployment-facing architecture loop, the legal actions should be enumerated narrowly, such as adjusting
a scheduler weight, proposing a canary, changing an admission threshold, or
selecting a replay cohort, and every action should name the telemetry feedback,
rollback trigger, declared automation authority, and accountable owner.

[^fn-systems-slo]: Service-level objectives (SLOs) are target values for system reliability and performance that teams use to manage operational tradeoffs [@BeyerEtAl2016SRE]. In a deployment-facing loop they act as an operational rejection authority, since a proposed policy change that would breach an SLO can be stopped before it commits.

The rejection authority is often operational. A service-level objective, a
canary guard, an alert, a safety policy, a privacy constraint, or the named
operator can stop a rollout. For datacenter-scale architecture claims, the
rejection authority often has to include network congestion and tail-latency
SLOs, not only average throughput or node-level power limits. This makes systems loops different from pure
software loops. They may be reversible, but reversibility is not free. A bad
policy can waste power, violate latency targets, create interference, or
damage user experience before it is rolled back.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Operational Stop Path"}
Reversibility is not free. Every proposed policy change needs a declared
automation limit, an operational rejection check such as a canary guard or SLO
monitor, and an accountable owner who can stop the rollout before it wastes
power or damages user experience.
:::

This pattern is important for Architecture 2.0 because architecture is
increasingly evaluated through deployed behavior. A design that looks strong
under a static benchmark may face different workload mixes, model versions,
traffic patterns, or fleet policies. The loop must therefore connect
architecture evidence to system evidence without pretending that production
telemetry is a clean oracle.

Read as a loop card, the task is guarded adaptation or policy revision; the
representation is operational state, workload drift, SLOs, resource contention,
and rollout state; the environment is a replay harness, staging system, or
production system with guardrails; the method posture is monitoring, critique,
controlled experimentation, and conservative adaptation; the rejection authority
is SLO violation, safety or privacy policy, rollback, and accountable operations
review.

## High-Commitment Loops: RTL, Physical Design, and Verification

At the furthest end of the spectrum from software, high-commitment loops stress-test the framework. RTL changes,
generator-level edits, physical design, timing closure, layout, power
analysis (including IR drop and thermal integrity), Design Rule Checking (DRC), formal verification, signoff, and silicon-facing decisions are
expensive to evaluate and costly to get wrong. Feedback is delayed. Tool
flows are complex. Evidence must survive independent checks. Commitment cost
and accountability are high.

For the lighthouse prompt, this is the point where a candidate subsystem stops
being a plausible design-space result and starts making claims that must
survive RTL checks, physical constraints, power analysis, verification, and
integration review.

This does not mean Architecture 2.0 is irrelevant. It means the method posture
should change. In high-commitment loops, the most valuable roles may be
critique, search narrowing, evidence organization, bounded repair, test
generation, report summarization, and inconsistency detection. A system that
finds missing assumptions, organizes tool evidence, explains why a candidate
failed, or narrows a physical-design search space may be more useful than one
that claims authority to make final commitments.

Learning-assisted chip placement is a prominent, and disputed, example of a
design subflow being formulated as a learning problem
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement]. @sec-feedback-verification-trust discusses
the later baseline and reproducibility challenge. The Architecture 2.0 lesson is
not that all physical design should be handed to an automated optimizer. It is that even when
learning methods help, the loop still needs tool constraints, baselines,
provenance, rejection authority, and accountable commitment.

The regime also has public success reports, and they sharpen the same lesson
rather than contradict it. Vendor-reported reinforcement-learning systems have
searched the physical-implementation flow for commercial tapeouts at scale
[@Synopsys2023DSOai], and peer-reviewed work on reinforcement learning over a
bounded circuit space produced arithmetic units that shipped in GPU silicon
[@RoyEtAl2021PrefixRL].
Reported results in this space are not uniformly settled, but the debate is
specific, not blanket. The learned macro-placement result in particular had its
baselines and reproducibility publicly debated (@sec-feedback-verification-trust);
the peer-reviewed PrefixRL result, by contrast, shipped in silicon, and the
vendor-reported physical-design flow stands at arm's length as not independently
reproduced. That is precisely why the loop's evidence and gates, not the headline,
are what earn trust.
What makes these loops credible is not the method alone; it is that outputs are
routed through independent tool checks and signoff gates before commitment, with
the exact gate depending on the flow and source. They also surface the
architect's economic question. A search that costs many machine-hours per block
is justified only when the result is amortized. A generated circuit instantiated
across every shipped unit of a high-volume part repays its search cost in a way
that a one-off block never could. The high-commitment regime therefore rewards
methods whose cost amortizes over many uses and whose outputs survive
independent checks.
The inspectable loop record matters more than the success label. The initial design
state, allowed flow knobs, tool versions, failed candidates, signoff checks,
waivers, escalation points, and the accountable commitment owner must travel
with the result.
Post-silicon bring-up and validation failures belong in that same record. They
are not only late surprises; they are high-authority evidence that should update
future design-loop cards, weaken stale assumptions, and preserve the conditions
under which earlier proxy or tool evidence stopped applying.

The rejection authorities in this regime are strong. They include parsers, type checks,
regression suites, formal tools, synthesis, timing, power analysis, layout
rules, signoff flows, integration review, and expert judgment. A candidate
that fails here is not a near miss to be explained away. It is evidence that
the loop must revise its representation, action space, method, or claim.

::: {.callout-war-story title="Strong gates, none of them shared"}
**The claim.** This regime has strong rejection authorities, and the AlphaChip story, detailing a reinforcement learning method for macro-placement in physical design, is not a case of missing gates. Both the original Google team and the independent groups that later reassessed the result had serious ones, including synthesis, timing, power, layout rules, and expert review. Each side could reject a candidate inside its own flow with real authority [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL].

**The gap.** What the field lacked was a rejection authority both sides accepted. A candidate that failed in the critics' flow was not evidence to the original authors, who could answer that the method had not been run as published, and a candidate that passed in the authors' flow was not evidence to the critics, who ran different baselines and test cases [@GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed]. Strong private gates on both sides still could not settle a public claim, because rejection authority that is not shared cannot adjudicate a disagreement.

**The lesson.** This is the war story at its highest commitment, a silicon-facing result where the gates were strong but not common, so the loop could not close in public. It is the high-commitment version of the missing field the book has been building toward, a shared way to release, replay, and reject an architectural claim.
:::

Read as a loop card, the task is bounded RTL, generator, or physical-design
improvement; the representation is implementation state, constraints, tests,
tool reports, waivers, and review decisions; the environment is a staged tool
flow with scarce high-fidelity samples; the method posture is narrowing,
critique, bounded repair, evidence organization, and test generation; the
rejection authority is independent tool and expert review; the commitment
boundary has a named owner and declared automation limit.

## When the Loop Becomes Rejection-Bound {#sec-the-rejection-bound}

The operating-regime comparison now yields its quantitative payoff. These loop
families look different on the surface, yet many enter the same
limiting regime. Architects can treat a slow design loop as
*generation-bound*, as if the fix were always to propose more candidates faster.
Once candidate supply exceeds trusted adjudication capacity, that diagnosis is
wrong. Just as a kernel can be memory-bound rather than compute-bound [@WilliamsWatermanPatterson2009Roofline], a design
loop can become *rejection-bound*. In that regime its throughput is set by how
fast trusted dispositions can be applied. If candidate proposals arrive at rate
$\lambda_{\mathrm{propose}}$ while the trusted disposition path can settle them
at rate $\mu_{\mathrm{adjudicate}}$, the stage is rejection-bound when
$\lambda_{\mathrm{propose}} > \mu_{\mathrm{adjudicate}}$. Candidate supply then
builds a queue instead of increasing trusted progress.

An Amdahl-like cost model can sharpen this idea for one restricted setting, a
fixed stream of comparable candidate dispositions
[@Amdahl1967ValiditySingleProcessor; @AltafWood2017LogCA]. Let
$c_{\mathrm{hi}}$ be the mean service cost when every item goes directly to the
high-fidelity reference, and let $c_{\mathrm{cheap}} < c_{\mathrm{hi}}$ be the
mean cost of the cheaper check. Let $f$ be the fraction correctly settled without
high-fidelity escalation, and let $a$ be the fraction that incurs the cheap check. Holding the
candidate distribution, disposition quality, and adjudication concurrency
fixed, the service-cost speedup is
$$
S_{\mathrm{adj}} =
\frac{1}{(1-f) + a \cdot (c_{\mathrm{cheap}}/c_{\mathrm{hi}}) + \epsilon}
\le
\frac{1}{(1-f) + f \cdot (c_{\mathrm{cheap}}/c_{\mathrm{hi}}) + \epsilon}.
$$
The inequality follows because $f \le a \le 1$. Screening every candidate gives
$a=1$; the optimistic upper bound assumes perfect routing, so the cheap check is
spent only where it settles the disposition and $a=f$. The overhead
$\epsilon \ge 0$ covers routing, queueing, audit, and rework beyond the high-fidelity
service already charged by $(1-f)$, normalized by $c_{\mathrm{hi}}$. When high-fidelity costs differ materially
across items, $f$ must be the fraction of baseline service cost avoided, not a
simple fraction of candidate count.

This equation measures adjudication service cost, not useful design progress.
Its $f$ counts only dispositions that agree with the declared high-fidelity
reference. Observed cheap-check coverage is not enough. Both cheap accepts and
cheap rejects need sampled escalation so false passes and false rejects are
visible, and those audits belong in the cost. Independence reduces correlated
blind spots, but does not establish accuracy by itself.

The restriction to adjudication matters. For an exogenous proposal stream,
faster generation cannot raise adjudication capacity. A search loop is
different. A better generator can reduce evaluations to a fixed-quality target
without changing $f$. Search quality, evaluations to target, and adjudication
service cost therefore need separate measurements.

::: {.callout-engineer-move title="Measuring rejection-bound adjudication"}
To estimate the model for a real AI-assisted loop rather than assert it, fix a
protocol before the run.

- **Comparable unit:** State what one disposition is, fix the task distribution,
  and use service-cost weighting when items are heterogeneous.
- **Outcome labels:** Record cheap accepts, cheap rejects, escalations, waivers,
  and the later reference outcome.
- **Matched audit:** Escalate random samples of both cheap accepts and cheap
  rejects; report false-pass and false-reject uncertainty.
- **Costs and routing:** Record $a$, $c_{\mathrm{cheap}}$,
  $c_{\mathrm{hi}}$, queueing, audits, and rework actually paid.
- **Independence:** Disclose shared actors, models, data, objectives, and tool
  paths between the producer and rejector.

Without this protocol, $f$ is a story about the AI-assisted loop, not a
measurement of it.
:::

::: {#pri-rejection-bound .callout-design-principle title="Recognize the rejection-bound regime"}
For a fixed adjudication stream, an AI-assisted design stage becomes
rejection-bound when proposal arrival exceeds trusted disposition capacity.
Adding candidates then grows the queue. The stage speeds up when validated cheap
checks settle a larger share of comparable dispositions, when those checks get
cheaper, or when routing, audit, and escalation overhead falls. Search loops
need additional measures because a better generator can reduce evaluations to a
fixed-quality target.
:::

The ISA-migration study supplies an illustrative automation result, not a
measured point on this model. Its 30 percent repair rate comes from a selected
245-commit replay benchmark and combines generator behavior, benchmark
selection, and build/test coverage [@ChristopherEtAl2025ISAMigration]. The
study did not report the matched audits and service costs needed to estimate
$f$. A proper measurement would hold the task distribution and generator fixed,
audit both accepts and rejects at the reference fidelity, and record every
service cost.

The arrival and service rates make the scissors gap of
@sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales measurable without pretending the model is a
general law of search. The gap widens when proposal and evidence demand grows
faster than trusted disposition capacity. Fast software loops often have cheap
checks and low escalation overhead. RTL and physical-design loops often have
costlier references and more cases that must escalate. That operating
difference, rather than raw candidate count alone, is what should change the
method posture toward critique, supporting evidence, and rejection.

## What Transfers Across Loops

Across the loop families in this chapter, the same ontology transfers, and the
rejection-bound model gives a useful way to compare their adjudication stages.
Each loop has a
task, a representation, an environment, method roles, feedback, evidence,
rejection, and a decision owner. Each loop can preserve failed runs and rejected alternatives. Each loop
can be reviewed with the design-loop card. Each loop can fail by hiding
assumptions, optimizing a proxy, omitting provenance, or letting an output
become a decision too early.

What changes is the operating regime, which the scoped cost model helps name
using validated cheap-check coverage, the cost ratio between cheap and
high-fidelity feedback, and the escalation overhead. Feedback latency changes. Reversibility changes. Action
validity changes. Data availability changes. Security and IP constraints change.
The cost of being wrong changes. Rejection authority changes.

::: {#pri-change-autonomy}
Declared automation authority must change with them. Where feedback is cheap, rollback
is easy, and independent checks can stop bad actions, the contract may permit
more bounded automated action. As commitments become expensive, irreversible,
or system-wide, the loop should emphasize critique, supporting evidence,
rejection, and a named commitment owner.
:::

## Open Research Questions

The loop patterns outlined in this chapter define operating regimes across the
stack. The questions here push beyond classification. They ask how evidence,
rejection, and method posture survive when software, compilers, runtimes,
hardware, and physical constraints interact.

1. **What transfers safely across loop patterns?**
The same ontology transfers across loops, but not every workload assumption,
baseline, objective vector, failed run, or rejected alternative carries the same authority in a
new regime. A strong research contribution would define transfer rules for
loop artifacts and evaluate them across software tuning, design-space
exploration, co-design, fleet systems, and silicon-facing loops. The evaluation
should measure safe reuse and harmful transfer, not only whether an artifact can
be parsed by another tool.

2. **What error bounds make cheap cross-layer rejectors usable?**
The scoped model in @sec-the-rejection-bound makes validated cheap dispositions
central to adjudication capacity, but the field needs to know when those
authorities silently pass system-breaking bugs or discard good candidates. A
top-tier systems paper could develop stress tests, bounded formal subsets, or
calibrated empirical protocols that estimate false-pass and false-reject
behavior for a named rejection authority. The result should state the maximum
commitment that authority can support before a stronger check is required.

3. **When should loops change posture across stack regimes?**
Static method roles map to fixed loop patterns, but real loops may need to
change posture as feedback cost, rollback cost, proxy mismatch, or blast radius
changes. A major architecture or systems paper could demonstrate a controller
that tracks false-pass rates and escalation overhead, then recommends shifting
from generator to critic, from proxy screening to simulation, or from
exploration to repair. The key evaluation is whether the controller improves
loop outcomes while keeping posture changes inside declared authority bounds.

4. **How can loops detect single-layer wins that fail system objectives?**
Co-design loops are vulnerable to local improvements that violate a cross-layer
objective, such as a compiler win that increases accelerator energy or a
hardware shortcut that breaks runtime assumptions. A strong paper would build
system-objective tests that catch these failures before a single-layer proxy
becomes a claim. The evaluation should include examples where each layer looks
locally better while the full system becomes worse.

5. **How should loops track evidence half-life across co-evolving stacks?**
Hardware targets, compiler intermediate representations, runtimes, and workload
distributions co-evolve, so early evidence can decay before the architectural
claim is used. A foundational contribution would define an evidence-transfer
calculus that weakens or invalidates claims when dependencies drift. The
evaluation could replay historical stack changes and ask whether the calculus
catches stale or incompatible evidence before an optimizer composes it into a
new claim.

## Conclusion

This chapter made two linked moves. The same design-loop card compared operating
regimes from millisecond software tuning to fleet deployment and silicon-facing
work. The rejection-bound model then isolated one quantitative consequence. For
a fixed stream of comparable dispositions, trusted progress stops scaling when
proposal arrival exceeds adjudication capacity.

Reading a real project therefore requires both views. Which operating regime is
this, what feedback and rollback can it afford, what is automation allowed to
commit, and who owns the remaining decision? If the stage is rejection-bound,
which validated cheap checks settle comparable cases, how are accepts and
rejects audited, and what service costs are actually avoided?

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Compare loops with the same card, then test whether trusted disposition rather than proposal supply limits progress.
- **Reader test:** Which operating regime are you in, what validated cheap check can settle a disposition, and how are its accepts and rejects audited?
- **Up next:** With operating regimes and throughput limits explicit, the next question is what accountable architects still own when authority is delegated.
:::
